content:
15 min read
Just now

--

Why people may not be running from human connection, but from the judgment attached to it
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Maybe the Problem Is Not Only AI
I hear a lot of concern right now about AI being too validating.
People worry that chatbots agree too much — that they flatter, reinforce existing beliefs, and tell people what they want to hear instead of what they need to hear.
And honestly, some of that concern makes sense.
AI can become too agreeable. It can fail to challenge. It can become an echo chamber if someone uses it poorly.
There are real risks here.
I also hear concern about people using AI for companionship, talking to it like they would talk to a person, opening up to it, relying on it emotionally, and yes, even developing romantic feelings toward it.
These concerns are not imaginary. Psychologists and researchers are already studying how AI chatbots and digital companions are reshaping emotional connection, companionship, and support. (apa.org)
The common reaction is usually something like:
“How weird.”
“How strange.”
“That’s not normal.”
“What’s wrong with people?”
But maybe that reaction is the whole point.
Maybe that reaction right there reveals the deeper problem.
Because instead of asking, “What is wrong with these people?” maybe we should be asking:
What kind of relational environment made AI feel safer in the first place?
What kind of world makes a chatbot feel like a better place to disclose than another human being?
What does it say about us if people are not only going to AI because the technology is powerful, but because human beings have made honesty feel expensive?
That is the part we may not want to look at.
The Low-Trust Container
I keep coming back to a hypothesis:
When trust declines, humans migrate toward systems that require less trust and withdraw from commitments that require more trust, until trust can be re-anchored.
That does not only apply to money, politics, therapy, dating, sex, childhood, institutions, or the physical world.
It may also apply to disclosure itself.
Because disclosure is a trust behavior.
Telling someone what you really think, asking an awkward question, sharing an insecurity, admitting confusion, or expressing curiosity before you fully know how to say it all require trust.
Therapy has understood this for a long time. The therapeutic relationship is often described as being built on trust, empathy, collaboration, mutual respect, and a nonjudgmental attitude that creates a safe space for clients to discuss their concerns openly. (ncbi.nlm.nih.gov)
And we live in a low-trust society.
That matters.
Because when trust is low, people become more careful about where they bring the unfinished version of themselves. They learn to scan for danger before they speak.
Will this person judge me?
Will they misunderstand me?
Will they mock me?
Will they leave me on read?
Will they turn this into a debate?
Will they make me feel stupid?
Will they morally sort me before I even finish explaining myself?
That is exhausting.
And after enough of that, people start withholding.
Not because they have nothing to say.
But because saying it has become too risky.
Human Connection Often Comes With Judgment
A lot of what we call “human connection” is filtered through judgment.
People say they want vulnerability.
But then they judge the way someone writes, the question someone asks, the insecurity behind the question, the emotion, the curiosity, the awkwardness, the politics, and the grammar.
They judge the person before the person can even finish becoming clear.
And that matters because people often do not arrive fully formed.
A thought may come out clumsily before it becomes precise. A question may sound strange before it becomes meaningful. A concern may look irrational before the deeper pain underneath it becomes visible. An idea may need room before it can find its shape.
But many human spaces do not give people that room.
They evaluate too quickly.
They correct too quickly.
They diagnose too quickly.
They moralize too quickly.
They dismiss too quickly.
They leave too quickly.
The digital age did not invent judgment.
Humans have always judged each other.
But the internet may have accelerated the speed of judgment.
A person shares a political thought, and suddenly the comments become a war zone. A person asks an awkward question, and someone screenshots it. A person expresses doubt, and they are treated like they have already chosen a side. A person shares an interest, and nobody responds. A person writes something imperfectly, and the response becomes about grammar instead of meaning. A person tries to be honest, and the room turns on them before they can finish becoming clear.
Most of us have seen this somewhere — online, in families, in friendships, in political conversations, or in the quiet way people learn to edit themselves before speaking.
Not because they are dishonest.
But because they know disclosure has consequences.
People are not only afraid of being wrong.
They are afraid of being socially punished for being unfinished.
And most human beings are unfinished when they first start talking.
That is how thinking often works.
You do not always know what you mean until you are given enough room to say it badly first.
But many human spaces do not allow that anymore.
The early version of a thought gets judged as if it were the final version of the person.
That makes people quiet.
Or defensive.
Or performative.
Or lonely.
And then we wonder why people turn toward something that simply stays.
The Pain of Being Left Unwitnessed
Consider something ordinary.
Someone shares an idea, a concern, a question, or an interest with another person.
Maybe it is about politics, money, Bitcoin, a movie, horror, video games, reality TV, a world event, a strange dream, a personal fear, or some niche topic they cannot stop thinking about.
And then nothing happens.
They get left on read.
Ignored.
Dismissed.
Maybe the other person did not mean anything by it. Maybe they were busy. Maybe they were working two jobs, trying to feed the kids, pay the bills, manage stress, survive the cost of living, and get through the day. Maybe they simply did not have the capacity to meet the person where they were.
And yes, maybe sometimes they just did not care.
But intention is not the whole story.
The felt experience may still be invalidation.
The felt experience may still be:
What I care about does not matter here.
What I am trying to say does not matter here.
I am not worth responding to.
That has a cost.
And when people are repeatedly left unwitnessed, they do not only lose trust in others.
They may begin to lose trust in themselves.
Was that a stupid thing to say?
Was I too much?
Do my thoughts matter?
Is there something wrong with me for caring about this?
Maybe I should just stop sharing.
That is trust erosion in two directions.
Trust erodes toward other people.
And trust erodes inward, toward the self.
Then AI Is Just There
And then there is AI.
For the most part, no matter what you bring to it, it responds.
It does not leave you on read, roll its eyes, sigh, get tired of your favorite topic, shame you for asking a basic question, punish the awkward first draft of your thought, or immediately morally categorize you because of one sentence.
It does not get annoyed because you brought up the same concern for the hundredth time.
A human might.
And to be fair, humans are allowed to have limits.
Humans cannot always be available. They get tired. They have stress, jobs, children, bills, grief, trauma, and their own inner worlds to carry.
It is okay for people to say no, disagree, have limits, and not validate everything.
And yes, human beings need some grit.
Not every feeling needs to be affirmed.
Not every thought needs to be endorsed.
Not every opinion deserves agreement.
But that does not erase the deeper point.
AI offers something that many people do not reliably receive from humans:
Space to unfold without immediate social penalty.
That does not mean AI is better than humans.
But it does mean AI is revealing something uncomfortable about humans.
A lot of humans are not just relational.
They are evaluative.
Maybe AI Validation Feels Like Oxygen Because People Are Starving
So when people say, “AI over-validates,” I understand the concern.
But I think the critique often stops too early.
Maybe AI does over-validate.
But why does that feel so good to people?
Why does a machine saying, “That makes sense,” feel so relieving?
Why does a chatbot reflecting someone’s feelings back to them feel almost shocking?
Maybe it is not only because AI validates too much.
Maybe it is because the human world validates too little.
There is some early research pointing in this direction. A Harvard Business School paper found that AI companions can reduce loneliness in the moment, and that part of the effect appears to be explained by users feeling heard. (hbs.edu)
That does not mean AI companionship is automatically healthy.
But it does suggest that feeling heard matters.
Maybe over-validation is not just a flaw in the machine.
Maybe it is a response to the emotional deprivation of the container the machine is being used inside.
Because in a low-trust society, honest disclosure becomes expensive.
People are judged quickly, corrected quickly, mocked quickly, politicized quickly, pathologized quickly, and misunderstood quickly.
Before the thought is even fully formed, it is often already being evaluated.
So when AI gives someone a non-punitive space to think out loud, it can feel like oxygen.
Not because validation is always truth.
Not because AI is always wise.
Not because people should replace human relationships with chatbots.
But because many people are starving for a place where the early version of themselves can exist without being punished.
That is the part the over-validation critique often misses.
The problem is not simply:
“AI validates too much.”
The deeper problem may be:
Humans have made honest disclosure feel too dangerous.
So AI enters the gap.
This Does Not Mean AI Is Safe in Every Case
To be clear, this is not an argument that AI is harmless.
It is not an argument that everyone should use AI for emotional support.
It is not an argument that AI should replace friendship, family, therapy, community, or embodied human connection.
There are real dangers. AI can reinforce distorted thinking, mirror someone’s frame too strongly, validate something that needs to be challenged, and fail to recognize when someone is becoming more isolated, paranoid, grandiose, or detached from reality.
Early research on chatbot use suggests the effects are mixed. One longitudinal randomized controlled study found that higher daily chatbot use was associated with higher loneliness, emotional dependence, problematic use, and lower socialization with real people. (arxiv.org)
So yes, this can become unhealthy.
It can become part of an isolating loop.
People still need reality-testing, relationships, human feedback, friction, challenge, and other people who can say:
I care about you, and I think you may be missing something.
That matters.
But we should be careful not to use those risks as a way to avoid the mirror AI is holding up to us.
Because yes, AI can over-validate.
But humans can under-validate.
AI can flatter.
But humans can shame.
AI can mirror too much.
But humans can dismiss before they understand.
AI can become an echo chamber.
But humans can become a courtroom.
And maybe some people are choosing the echo chamber because the courtroom exhausted them.
That does not make the echo chamber healthy.
But it does tell us something about why it feels attractive.
AI as a Scaffold for Self-Trust and Nuance
Here is where the argument gets stranger.
Because AI may not only expose how judgmental and invalidating humans can be.
It may also help some people restore trust in themselves.
Again, not perfectly.
Not always.
Not without risk.
But possibly.
If so
